Hedge Funds Focus on Refined Fuel Shortages

Investors remained very bearish about the outlook for petroleum prices before the latest upsurge in attacks on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and the breakdown of the ceasefire between the United States and Iran.

But the overall bearish positioning concealed a growing divergence between extreme bearishness on crude and a more neutral or even bullish outlook for refined products, especially U.S. gasoline and European gasoil.

Crude markets remain oversupplied in the short term following a sharp rise in tanker transits through the Strait in recent weeks, though that could change quickly if the waterway is reclosed.

But inventories of refined fuels have been severely depleted since the start of the war between the United States and Iran.

The recent intensification of Ukraine’s long-range attacks on Russia’s oil refineries threatens to make fuel shortages worse.

Russia’s refineries mostly produce and export diesel and other middle distillates rather than gasoline but the loss of refining capacity will tighten markets for all fuels worldwide.

Refineries in North America, Europe and Asia forced to produce more diesel will be able to make less jet fuel and gasoline ...

THE VOL PICTURE

All month, traders paid for protection against a crash and refused to let it go. They kept their hedges through the strikes on Iran, through the Fed Minutes, and even through Thursday's rally when a peace deal looked possible. Then on Friday afternoon they finally sold them. The VIX dropped 0.81 to 15.03, and unlike earlier in the week, this decline came from real selling, not mechanical noise. Even the deep crash protection that stayed bid through every headline this month was let go. Think about that timing for a second.

Then the US struck Iran on Sunday.

The market dropped its guard at exactly the wrong moment. Options are now the cheapest they have been in weeks. Monday is priced for a very quiet day, and even Tuesday's CPI morning is priced cheap, even though the market has been making much bigger moves than that lately. Two things can happen from here. If the weekend strikes turn out to matter, everyone will rush to buy protection at the same time, and that rush will make the move bigger. If the strikes do not matter, the quiet grind simply continues and the cheap options were right. Keep one number in mind either way. A single day where SPX moves 113 points or more, in either direction, changes this whole calm picture.

One more thing, the bigger picture behind the week. Last week alone about 56 billion dollars went into stocks, one of the largest weekly amounts this year, and tech funds are headed for their biggest year of inflows ever. Meanwhile the cash parked in money market funds just hit its highest level ever. That is a huge pile of money waiting to buy every dip, and it is a big part of why the market recovers so fast. But it also means most people are already in. From here, rallies tend to get smaller since there is less new money left to push prices up. And if a bad surprise hits, everyone is sitting on gains with no hedges, so they all rush to sell at the same time. That is how small dips turn into sharp ones. History says a setup like this usually leads to a flat or slightly weaker market over the next two to three months, not a crash.

There is also an early warning sign worth knowing about. Trouble usually shows up in the riskiest stocks first, and it already has. Small Korean tech stocks, the wildest corner of this whole rally, have dropped 36 percent in just eight weeks. The US indexes sit at record highs, but the edges of the market are already cracking, and the big names are always the last to fall.

So keep three simple things on your watch list for the summer.

First, watch the biggest tech stocks. They are the backbone of this rally. As long as they hold their long term uptrends, investors will treat every dip as a buying chance and the market stays supported.

Second, watch oil and bond yields. If both start rising together, it means inflation pressure is building again. That pushes the Fed toward a rate hike, and rate hikes are bad news for stocks. This is the combination that can end the quiet grind.

Third, watch Tuesday's CPI. If the number comes in hot, remember that traders sold their hedges on Friday. A hot print sends all of them rushing to buy that protection back at the same time, and that rush by itself can push the market down hard and fast.

WHAT HAPPENED FRIDAY AND THE WEEKEND

SPX closed Friday at 7575.39, up 31.75, finishing a winning week at plus 1.24 percent. Tech did the lifting. Meta jumped 6 percent to 669 on cloud news. Apple hit a record high and sued OpenAI over trade secrets. SK Hynix rose 13 percent in its Nasdaq debut and the chairman said demand is enormous, the chip trade passed its sentiment test loudly. Moderna fell 11 percent. The 10 Year sat at 4.56.

Then the weekend. Iran attacked a container ship in the Strait of Hormuz and the US launched airstrikes against Iran on Sunday. Trump separately threatened to decimate Iran if it tries to kill him. The Thursday deal hope is dead. And here is the tell: oil fell anyway, WTI at 71.51, down almost 1 percent. A month ago this headline was worth a 2 percent oil spike. The market is going numb to the strikes, which either means the escalation premium is done, or the market is asleep at exactly the wrong time. Tuesday decides.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR MONDAY

Monday is a positioning day in front of Tuesday's referee. The calendar is quiet, the straddle is small at ±34, and the board above spot is thin. The path above is nearly empty. Between the current price at 7575 and 7610 there is very little resistance. Even the 7600 round number, which usually acts as a wall, has buyers at it rather than sellers this time. The first real sellers show up at 7610 and again at 7620. So on a calm day the drift higher can continue toward 7609, where three things stack: the straddle top, the +1 ATR target at 7612, and the sellers. That zone is Monday's ceiling.

On the way down, the first slowdown comes around 7545 and 7530, where large positions sit and dips tend to stall. If the market gets there, expect a bounce attempt first. But if 7530 gives way, the next stretch is fast. There is very little between 7525 and 7505, so that part of a drop moves quickly. The level that actually matters is 7500. That is the strong support of the day. Right below it, at 7495, traders bought heavy protection over the weekend after the strikes, and it is now the biggest position on Monday's board. Here is the simple way to use those two numbers. As long as SPX stays above 7500, nothing bad has happened. If 7495 breaks, the drop gets fast, running through 7490 and 7475 with little in the way until buyers step in at 7460 and 7450. Everything below that, the 7420 to 7400 floor and the huge positions at 7350 and 7325, only comes into play if the war truly escalates. That is not the plan for Monday, it is the map for the worst case.

The honest Monday read: dips get bought until proven otherwise, money keeps arriving, and the tape wants to drift. But everyone is leaning the same way into a binary Tuesday morning with cheap protection. Trade the drift, keep it small, and do not carry size into the CPI.

Global financial markets are entering the new trading week with heightened caution. The dramatic collapse of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire agreement and subsequent military engagements in the Persian Gulf have fundamentally altered the commodity and risk-asset landscape. Markets are now pricing in a sustained "high-risk" premium, shifting focus from pure AI-speculation to defensive hedging and geopolitical energy resilience.
1. Geopolitical & Energy Crisis: Hormuz Remains Stalled
The Strait of Hormuz—the world’s most critical maritime energy artery—remains effectively paralyzed following the military escalation that began on July 9.
Maritime Logistical Impact: Global energy underwriters and shipping firms have largely halted transits through the Strait. Lloyd’s of London has increased war-risk insurance premiums for the Persian Gulf region by an additional 150% over the last 48 hours.
The Energy "Floor": Brent crude closed the week at approximately $78.20/bbl, marking a significant floor for oil prices. Energy analysts at Goldman Sachs and other desks are projecting that Brent could breach the $85/bbl mark early this week if diplomatic channels fail to reopen the waterway by Tuesday.
Global Strategic Reserve Status: With U.S. and European strategic petroleum reserves at multi-year lows, the market lacks a "buffer" to mitigate these physical supply disruptions, leaving energy prices highly sensitive to further regional escalations.
2. Global Equity & Treasury Outlook
The "AI-led" bull market, which defined the first half of 2026, is currently facing its stiffest test as institutional capital rotates into "Hard Asset" security.
Rotation to Defense: Equity strategists report a notable acceleration of capital out of highly levered "growth" technology stocks and into defensive sectors, particularly Aerospace & Defense, Global Utilities, and Food/Ag-Commodity producers.
Flight to Safety: The U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield is expected to open lower (trading at ~4.42% in pre-week indicative pricing) as investors prioritize the liquidity and stability of U.S. sovereign debt over corporate equity risk.
The "Warsh" Fed Context: Market focus remains fixed on how Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh will reconcile his "binary, data-dependent" monetary framework with the sudden supply-side energy shock. If energy prices continue to spike, the Fed may be forced into a more aggressive, inflation-fighting posture, even in the face of a softening labor market.
3. Notable Weekend Headlines
Iran’s Response: Over the weekend, Tehran signaled that it would seek "regional solidarity" to exert economic pressure on the West, raising concerns that the conflict may spread to involve pipeline infrastructure in neighboring Gulf states.
Corporate Tech Resilience: Despite the geopolitical friction, enterprise software remains a bright spot. Companies providing automated cybersecurity and AI-driven logistics optimization tools are seeing an uptick in inquiries, as global enterprises rush to harden their supply chains against digital and physical disruption.
Asian Indices Pre-Open: Futures markets in Tokyo and Seoul indicate a cautious start for Monday. Asian traders are particularly concerned about regional energy import costs, which are already showing up in inflationary forecasts for the third quarter.

Every rally is framed in advance as a relief rally. The consensus target is a Q4 bottom followed by the next cycle. Last year the same community said October 2025 would mark the beginning of the real run. It did not play out as expected. 

Now October 2026 is the new consensus target.

When parabolic structures in the indices break, the phase that follows is typically volatile for weeks before the actual corrective trend asserts itself. 

In that window, the majority believes the run is continuing, goes all-in at maximum risk, and then the real decline begins. 

The question of how crypto starts a new cycle while traditional markets are simultaneously entering their post-parabola corrective phase has no historical precedent. 

Crypto is a small asset class relative to equities. It needs only a fraction of the capital that moves through traditional markets to produce outsized moves. 

The bullish divergence that has been building between equities and crypto will probably resolve. The direction of that resolution has been the core of this analysis for months.

MACRO WINDOW

Tuesday is the most important single day of the week with three simultaneous events: CPI at 8:30 ET, Warsh before Congress for his first semi-annual monetary policy testimony, and the major banks reporting before the open.

Consensus expects headline CPI to print approximately -0.1% month-over-month for June, which would clip the annual rate from 4.2% to approximately 3.9%, driven by an estimated 9.2% decline in gasoline prices following the Hormuz reopening. 

Core CPI is expected to rise 0.3% month-over-month, keeping the annual core rate steady at 2.9%. The headline may generate “inflation is cooling” narratives, but the Fed focuses on core, which remains sticky.

May CPI came in at 4.2% YoY, driven almost entirely by a 23.5% surge in energy costs from the Iran conflict.

Since then, Brent crude has fallen from approximately 120 dollars to 70 dollars, a 42% decline. 

The energy component that drove the recent PCE and CPI readings is already reversing. 

Europe confirmed this pattern: Eurozone CPI fell to 2.8% in June, Germany to 2.3%, France to 2.0%. Not because of rate hikes, but because oil fell. The US follows with a lag.

Consumer inflation expectations for one year ahead rose to 3.7% in June, the highest since September 2023. But gas price growth expectations fell 3.5 points to just 1.5%, the lowest since August 2022. The bond market reflects the same ambiguity. 

10-year at 4.561% (+1.79% wk), 
2-year at 4.212% (+1.86% wk), 
1-year at 4.06% (+2.55% wk). 

Yields are elevated but have moved in a narrow range this week. The market is waiting for Tuesday's data before committing.

Polymarket prices a 78% probability of no change at the July 29 FOMC meeting and 22% for a 25bp hike. 

Futures markets price the fed funds rate rising toward 3.8% by October and approaching 4% by year-end. 

The market remains too hawkish in my view. 

The inflation driving current readings is a supply shock, not demand-driven. Raising rates against a supply shock is the same structural error the ECB made. A softer CPI on Tuesday would begin to reprice the September hike odds and could act as the catalyst for the next equity leg higher.

Oil at 71.50, +4.49% on the week, a modest recovery after Friday's renewed Iran tension headlines. Trump said the ceasefire with Iran is "over." IEA meanwhile reported that global oil demand is set to fall for the first time since 2020.

The Q2 season is broadly expected to show 23% EPS growth year over year, the second strongest quarter since 2021. Strong numbers will fuel the narrative further. But strong earnings have never extended a cycle when the structure is already turning.

RISK POSITIONING AND WHAT IT MEANS

This week's dedicated posts covered the positioning in detail. 

A brief summary: put/call skew at its lowest reading in recorded history. Fund managers at their lowest cash allocation ever. $5.4 billion into SOXX in a single day, the highest since 2001. $1.2 billion into SOXL on the same day. 48% of all retail options volume in 0DTE contracts.

The top 20 S&P 500 performers in the first half of 2026 averaged gains that would normally take years to accumulate. 

SanDisk +857% year-to-date, nearly 4,000% since its February 2025 IPO. 

Micron +304%, 
Intel +278%, 
Western Digital +270%. 

The majority of these gains were concentrated in the April to June window, the exact parabolic phase we have been tracking in the Dot-Com fractal analysis. 

These are not market cycles. These are compression events. And what parabolas compress on the way up, they tend to release at the same speed on the way down. 

Gold fell 14% in a single session after its January peak. Silver fell 39% in a single day. The KOSPI has swung more than 25% in each direction over five weeks. 

The SOXX has already demonstrated the same pattern. 

The question is not whether the broader indices will follow. The question is timing.

─────────────────

PSYCHOLOGY AND MARKET MECHANICS

There is a reason major fractals keep repeating. Not because markets mechanically follow the same path, but because human behavior under identical emotional conditions remains nearly unchanged across centuries. Fear and greed rotate in relative predictable sequences. Technology changes. People do not.

When the next leg higher comes, and the structure points toward one, it will receive more media attention than the April to June move. 

Analysts are already projecting the S&P to 9,000 and beyond. Supercycle narratives in crypto are growing louder. This is not coincidental. It is the psychological mechanics of a blow-off top. In the final phase, the voices that warn grow quieter not because they are wrong but because the majority has stopped listening.

What follows is equally predictable. 

In October 2025, the entire crypto space was positioned for the start of the real run. 

The majority was long. 
The consensus was certain. 

What came next was the opposite. 

When the consensus is positioned in the same direction with high conviction, it is usually wrong precisely because of that positioning.

Today in traditional markets the data shows the same setup. Everyone is piling into the top performers of the last weeks and months, the already overextended trades, rather than into the breadth that is actually building. 

And in crypto, the consensus is now unanimously positioned for a Q4 bottom below 50,000, waiting to buy the supercycle entry. 

That narrative is already being amplified by large and well-known accounts, supported by arguments that AI momentum will spill into crypto, that institutional adoption is accelerating, and that government support adds structural tailwind. 

These are not wrong arguments. They will be the exact narratives that accompany the next leg higher and make most people blind to the actual phase we are in. 

The music always sounds best right before it stops.

That is the moment to watch for. Not when everyone is bearish. When everyone is certain.

─────────────────

BOTTOM LINE

The S&P is 0.6% from its all-time high. RSP is already there. The Dot-Com fractals are holding. The bullish divergence between equities and crypto continues to build. Tuesday's CPI could be the next catalyst.

The risk positioning data tells a clear story: the parabola is in its final phase. When it ends, the initial corrective move will be larger and faster than 2000 or 2007 because the leverage is larger and the synthetic exposure is greater. 

The A-wave after the top will reach the former breakout zone, most will treat the subsequent B-wave recovery as the dip-buying opportunity of the decade, and the C-wave will complete the structure near the late March 2026 lows. 

That sequence has played out twice before, in 2000 and in 2007, and the setup today is structurally more extended than either.

We are not there yet. The structure says one more leg higher first.

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

Brent: surplus spooks the bulls

• Goldman Sachs expects an oil market surplus of 3 million BPD in 2027.

• Without Warsh’s help, Japan will be forced to resort to currency interventions.

The Dollar is consolidating ahead of Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra, Portugal, and the release of key US labour market data. A hawkish surprise from the new Fed Chair at the June FOMC meeting sent financial markets into a tailspin, leading to a rise in Treasury yields and the greenback. If the central bank chief hints that he was misunderstood, everything will be turned on its head.

Meanwhile, the strengthening of the dollar has been one of the reasons behind the yen’s fall to 40-year lows and Brent’s rapid return to pre-Middle East conflict levels. In the spring, the rise in oil prices drove the dollar higher, but now the strengthening dollar is putting downward pressure on oil prices. That said, there are already plenty of headwinds for ‘black gold’.

Since the deal with the US was concluded, Iran has exported around 40 million barrels. Coupled with the rise in Russian oil supplies to 4.13M BPD, the highest since the beginning of 2022, this is exerting significant downward pressure on Brent.

Despite flare-ups in the conflict in the Middle East, tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues. The resumption of operations there allows Goldman Sachs to forecast a 3M BPD surplus in the oil market by 2027. Of this, 1 million BPD will be used to replenish strategic reserves. However, the remaining figure will be sufficient to give crude oil bears confidence.

The only thing likely to unsettle them would be the resumption of large-scale US air strikes against Iran. Donald Trump has not ruled out such action, but favours diplomacy.

Meanwhile, the USDJPY has exceeded 162 for the first time since 1986. Investors are bracing for a resumption of currency interventions, on which Japan spent over $70 billion in April and May. According to Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, the government and the central bank are ready to take appropriate measures regarding currencies at any time as necessary. The official noted that her US counterpart, Scott Bessent, had agreed with this.

If Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra fails to halt the US dollar’s advance, Japan will have no choice but to intervene in the forex market.

Summary: A strong dollar and rising supply are weighing on Brent; Japan is preparing to intervene amid a fall in the yen. (FxPro)

At the start of the day on 1 July, Bitcoin slipped to $57.8K, having recovered to above $59K at the time of writing. At its lowest point, BTC hit its lowest level since September 2024, and the subsequent attempt to rebound still looks rather tentative. The leading cryptocurrency is gradually slipping below the 200-week moving average, which is typical behaviour for it at this point on the curve, judging by several past instances.

Bitcoin fell by 20.5% in June to $58.6K, marking its second consecutive month of decline following a two-month rally. This monthly decline was the steepest in the last four years. From a seasonal perspective, July is generally considered a fairly successful month for BTC. Over the past 15 years, Bitcoin has ended the month higher on ten occasions and lower on five. The average gain was 19%, while the average decline was 7.8%.

News Background

Over the past week, BitMine purchased an additional 27,084 ETH worth $43 million, bringing the total amount of Ethereum in its reserves to 5.7 million ETH, which exceeds 4.7% of the total Ethereum supply. BitMine has achieved 94% of its target to consolidate 5% of the second-largest cryptocurrency's total supply over the past 11 months, according to BitMine's Chairman, Tom Lee.

According to Arkham Intelligence, the US company Sharplink has resumed buying Ethereum after an eight-month hiatus. Last week, the company acquired 39,196 Ether for $62.4 million.

Strategy did not buy any Bitcoin last week, but sold a block of MSTR ordinary shares, increasing its dollar reserves to $2.55 billion. According to the company, these funds will be sufficient to cover 17 months of expected share-based payments.

Following the latest adjustment, the Bitcoin mining difficulty rose sharply by 7.15% to 133.87 T. This is the largest increase in the metric since February.

According to SoSoValue, investors withdrew $4.3B from US Bitcoin ETFs in June. This monthly outflow was the largest since the launch of the Bitcoin ETF in January 2024.

Summary: The crypto market is at lows, with sentiment in the ‘fear’ zone. BTC fell by 20% in June and is trading around $59K, under pressure from selling and ETF outflows, despite a localised recovery. (FxPro)

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

Gold

$4,000: Just a pause, not the end of Gold's decline

Gold is rebounding at the end of the week after a correction in the odds of two key Fed rate hikes this year. Nevertheless, the price is falling for the fourth consecutive week. Since the second half of the week, bears have been relentlessly trying to push the price below the psychologically significant $4,000 mark but eased their grip on Friday afternoon. At the same time, the long-term moving averages favour sellers, as a ‘death cross’ is looming (when the 50-day moving average falls below the 200-day moving average), reinforced by the fact that the price is trading below this cross. On the weekly charts, attempts to push the price back above the 50-week moving average have failed. However, this is also the price support zone from the end of last year, so we can expect a fairly fierce battle around the current level.

It is also worth noting how the narrative for gold’s price movements has changed. Previously, the escalation between the US and Iran triggered downward price movements, with a clear inverse correlation between oil and gold. But it has now become apparent that both assets can move in the same direction – downwards.

However, we must also consider an alternative scenario, in which, since the end of January, the price of gold has been correcting following three years of growth, reaching the 61.8% level—a classic Fibonacci support level. Under this scenario, we should see consolidation followed by a reversal to the upside, with the first stage being a recovery to peak levels around $5,600, and the second stage a rally to $8,000 as part of an extension to the 161.8% level. We nevertheless view this undoubtedly inspiring scenario as less likely, given the history of previous similar price booms and crashes over the last half-century. (FxPro)

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

Sentiment: Speculative excess continues building: record $1.42 trillion in margin debt, up 53.7% year over year, a $26.5 billion IPO pop, SpaceX entering the Nasdaq-100 months after the largest listing ever, and narrow mega-cap leadership while the average stock stalls. About 63% of S&P 500 stocks remain above their 50-day average, but the equal-weight index lagged the cap-weight by 150 basis points this week. Gold held near $4,113. Crypto sentiment continues improving with a green July, resilience through a broken ceasefire, ETF inflows returning, and bottom calls becoming more common. The market’s ability to ignore the ceasefire collapse is either strength or complacency, and record margin debt suggests investors have already picked a side.

Last week I said the second-half setup was mid-July CPI showing the energy unwind and bank earnings testing the rotation. Both arrive Tuesday morning, and the setup has sharpened into a genuine collision. The S&P 500 sits 0.6% from an all-time high, leaning on the narrowest leadership since May, funded by record margin debt, and betting CPI prints 3.5%. The bond market has sold off 8 of 9 sessions because the Cleveland Fed model says 4%, while oil just bounced 5% on a dead ceasefire. Both markets cannot be right, and Tuesday is the reconciliation. The banks report the same morning and show whether the consumer behind June’s 57,000 jobs is still spending or quietly cracking. Crypto also showed something new: it held its range through a war escalation, whales remain loaded near the lows, ETH is close to confirming its turn, and July remains green. If CPI comes in cool, the S&P breaks to new highs, the Fed regains its easing path, and the crypto bottom callers look early instead of wrong. If Cleveland’s 4% is right, yields push toward 4.75%, narrow leadership loses its cushion, and record margin debt unwinds the way it always does: fast. One number, Tuesday at 8:30 a.m. The whole summer is riding on the second decimal place.

《2026年北美国别市场观察报告》

2025年美加墨（USMCA）三国贸易总额约1.59万亿美元，贸易节奏深度协同。墨西哥已取代加拿大及中国，成为美国第一大贸易伙伴。美国能源供给与制造业高度依赖邻国，加拿大提供美炼油厂23%的原油摄入量，墨西哥则承接了美国近一半的机动车零配件出口。宏观层面，美国通胀2.7%、失业率4.4%，加拿大通胀2.2%、失业率6.8%。AI基础设施投资驱动明显，罗素3000相关企业营收预期较2023年3月累计增加3000亿美元，AI投资占GDP峰值预期为2%。约49%的美国公司已将IT与软件开发近岸外包至加墨地区，北美近岸远程人才规模快速增长。

政策架构呈现“美国优先制度化”与“加拿大对冲性补贴”的双轨特征。美国通过《芯片与科学法案》（专项527亿美元）、《通胀削减法案》及《大而美法案》构建半导体与清洁能源补贴屏障，且CFIUS审查已扩围至AI、量子及能源等敏感领域，并针对特定对手（含中国）实施严厉准入限制。加拿大则通过战略响应基金（SRF）、战略创新基金（SIF）及加拿大增长基金（CGF）应对关税冲击，提供高比例成本报销，以撬动低碳技术与先进制造投入。美墨定于2026年5月启动USMCA首轮自动审议谈判，关键争议涉及原产地规则及关键矿产供应链安全。

产业布局上，中国企业倾向采用“中国研发部件+墨西哥组装+北美销售”的三角模式，选址向墨西哥北部蒙特雷集聚。信息技术市场预计年复合增长率（CAGR）为7.81%，智能制造市场规模预计2030年达857.2亿美元。金融科技领域预计CAGR为13.9%。中国企业在墨产能布局（如海信产能1000万台）虽享受零关税，但受EAR管制及337调查风险影响。未来USMCA审议可能收紧原产地规则，将直接威胁现有供应链合规性，企业需在谈判前完成原产地链路审计与合规风险对冲。

【关键词】：#USMCA #近岸外包 #原产地规则 #CFIUS #芯片与科学法案 #IRA #大而美法案 #SRF #SIF #CGF #美墨加谈判 #AI投资 #劳动生产率 #罗素3000 #蒙特雷 #海辰储能 #海信墨西哥 #美的北美 #智能制造 #布伦特油价 #EIA预测 #金融科技 #CETA #CPTPP #供应链审计 #合规风险 #EAR管制 #337调查 #近岸外包成本 #北美出海

【观点】：

报告属于典型的信息汇编型研究，数据源涵盖WTO、美国普查局、高盛及德勤等，可信度中等偏上。然而，必须区分“事实引用”与“投行假设”。高盛关于“生成式AI十年内提升美国劳动生产率15%且GDP增量4.5万亿美元”的预测属于情景假设，并非已实现事实，若投资者直接将其作为决策依据，面临极高的模型误差风险。

三个核心变量决定了北美出海的成败：一是USMCA 2026年审议的潜在不确定性，原产地规则收紧将直接击穿中国企业在墨组装的“免税合规”逻辑，该风险是当前最大的单点失效点，报告虽提及但权重过低。二是AI资本支出（Capex）的传导节奏，高盛预判的“峰值—回落”曲线意味着算力基建需求可能面临降速，加墨地区作为外包承接地，将经历从算力配套向软件应用侧的转型，企业需提前布局业务迁移路径。三是美欧关税僵局的外溢效应，美国对欧汽车加征25%关税若落地，势必导致欧洲车企竞相在北美（尤其是墨西哥）构建产能，从而挤占中国企业现有的集群空间与资源，这是报告未充分探讨的二阶影响。

逻辑盲点显著：报告将“近岸外包成本比美国低80%”泛化至加墨市场，这存在严重的统计口径误导。80%的成本优势仅适用于中美洲或加勒比海更低端的离岸人力，墨西哥软开成本实际仅比美国低约35%-50%。此外，报告未提示CFIUS对“穿透审查”的风险，即当中国背景企业在墨工厂的中方持股比例超过美方下游客户的合规阈值时，即便产品合规，依然会因下游客户的主动避险而被剔除出供应链。

抽象层级需提升至“供应链地缘政治”高度：北美出海已从简单的“制造外迁”演变为“合规身份管理”。企业不再仅仅是卖产品，而是在美墨边境通过资本合规与资产所有权穿透，管理一种“身份资产”。决策含义在于：企业必须将“地缘政治条款”写入商业合同，特别是针对原产地规则突变的成本分摊与解约机制。对能源侧而言，EIA预测的布伦特原油均价下行周期，隐含全球油市过剩假设，若OPEC+政策博弈超预期，原油价格波动将直接反噬墨西哥清洁能源项目的投资回报率。企业需在战略规划中引入敏感性测试，而非直接采纳投行基准预测。

五、本周前瞻

下周将迎来美股二季度财报季正式开启。汇丰银行数据显示，分析师对标普500成分股的盈利预期已上调至同比增长22%，为疫情后最高水平。同时，6月CPI数据将揭示油价回落是否已传导至消费者端。

整体来看，AI主线仍是当前市场核心驱动力，但美联储对通胀的鹰派表态与即将到来的财报季，或将为市场增添新的不确定性。

text:
全线拉升！美联储重磅报告落地，美股AI交易全面回归

美东7月10日晚间，美股三大指数集体走高，标普500逼近历史新高，AI科技赛道重回市场主线；叠加美联储发布新任主席首份半年度货币政策报告、SK海力士完成史上最大海外ADR上市两大重磅事件，全球算力产业链迎来双向催化。

一、美股收盘盘面复盘：AI巨头全线走强，存储板块集体回暖

1. 三大指数收盘数据
道琼斯工业平均指数收涨0.29%，纳斯达克指数上涨0.29%，标普500大涨0.42%，市场风险偏好明显修复，资金集中回流科技成长赛道。
2. 头部AI巨头涨跌分化，算力龙头领涨
大涨标的：Meta暴涨近6%，机构SemiAnalysis上调其AI算力基建评级；英伟达大涨超4%，HBM存储紧缺逻辑持续支撑估值；微软、特斯拉小幅收红；苹果、谷歌、亚马逊震荡微涨。
调整标的：SpaceX下跌超4%，短期资金兑现离场。
3. SK海力士ADR上市引爆存储板块，全产业链跟涨
韩国存储龙头SK海力士以临时代码SKHYV登陆纳斯达克，首日盘中最高涨幅接近19%，收盘大涨超12%，265亿美元融资规模创下海外企业赴美上市纪录。
受龙头上市情绪带动，美股存储标的集体走强：闪迪大涨超3%，希捷科技涨超2%，西部数据上涨0.78%；仅美光科技小幅收跌1.24%，跌幅大幅收窄。
市场共识：SK海力士美股上市，强化全球资金对AI算力、存储基础设施长期扩张的信心，HBM紧缺周期逻辑再次被资金定价。

二、SK海力士高层重磅发声：存储短缺贯穿至2030年后，两大长期布局落地
1. CEO郭鲁正（Kwak Noh-Jung）周期预警
上市采访中，SK海力士CEO给出行业最激进紧缺判断：2027年将是全球存储芯片史上供应最紧张的一年，即便公司持续加码扩产，AI驱动的需求增长将持续超过产能上限，供不应求格局延续至2030年以后。
目前下游云厂商、AI服务器企业已大规模签订长期锁价合同，侧面印证行业长期缺货预期。
2. 两大扩张与商业模式规划
海外建厂：不排除在美国新建存储晶圆基地，选址严格考核电力、水资源、高端芯片人才三大硬性指标；
全新商业模式：集团正在推进内存即服务，客户可按需租赁存储资源，不用一次性采购芯片，直接缓解AI企业短期算力内存扩容瓶颈。
3. SK集团会长崔泰源补充表态
AI算力需求将呈指数级爆发；若本次美股上市反馈超预期、投资者需求旺盛，公司不排除后续增发ADR进一步拓宽海外融资渠道，加码全球扩产。

三、美联储半年度货币政策报告（新任主席首份报告），核心要点拆解
美东7月10日美联储官网正式发布提交国会的半年度货币政策报告，这是凯文·沃什上任后首份完整政策报告，下周周二、周三他将赴参众两院接受听证，市场高度关注其鹰鸽立场。
1. 通胀现状：三大因素持续推高物价，AI首次被列为通胀推手
报告明确，今年春季美国通胀再度抬升，5月PCE物价指数仍接近2%目标两倍，三大持续性涨价压力：
美国关税政策滞后传导，消费品成本持续上行；
中东地缘冲突扰动能源供给，原油涨价向下游全产业链传导；
AI数据中心基建热潮，芯片、电力、高端原材料需求激增，直接形成持续性价格压力。
高盛经济学家Megan Peters量化测算：AI带来内存涨价、软件订阅提价、数据中心电价上行三重效应，当前已抬升美国核心PCE同比0.2个百分点，预计2026年底通胀贡献将扩大至0.5个百分点 。
2. 通胀数据分化：存在“温和降温”分项指标
美联储提出差异化通胀观察维度：达拉斯联储截尾平均PCE（剔除极端波动价格）从去年5月2.6%回落至今年5月2.4%，说明通胀内部结构分化，并非全线失控，为政策预留缓冲空间。
3. 经济与就业：AI托底增长，劳动力市场趋于平衡
制造业、工厂产出大幅走强，核心驱动力是全球AI数据中心设备采购潮，美国劳动生产率稳步提升；
就业端6月失业率4.2%，处于历史低位，劳动力供需基本平衡，无大面积失业或用工极度紧缺风险；
海外经济2026上半年增长疲软，中东冲突、美国关税形成外部逆风，但AI跨国投资部分对冲下行压力。
4. 美联储政策底线：承诺动用全部工具稳物价
报告重申双重使命目标（充分就业+价格稳定），明确表态：美联储准备动用全套货币政策工具调控通胀，若联邦基金利率触及下限，将启动非常规政策手段压制物价。当前市场一致定价，年内存在加息可能性。
四、行情深层逻辑总结与后市观察要点
短期行情驱动
SK海力士上市强化HBM存储稀缺叙事，叠加美联储报告确认AI长期资本开支景气，资金回流AI算力、存储、光模块赛道；虽然AI短期推升通胀带来加息预期，但市场更看重产业长期增长空间，科技股走出独立上涨行情。
中期两大核心变量
美联储主席沃什下周国会听证会发言，若释放偏鹰加息信号，会压制科技成长估值；若温和表态，AI行情将延续；
存储产能周期：2027年全球存储新厂集中投产，短期紧缺利好芯片企业，但2027-2028年供给放量或扭转行业盈利周期，需持续跟踪长单定价数据。
产业链机会传导
美股存储大涨将正向传导A股HBM、存储设备、特种气体、光通信板块；商业航天、创新药作为存量资金高低切换赛道，仍具备结构性机会。

text:
7月10日美股复盘：指数收在高位，广度继续扩张，期权结构却退回临界状态

市场正在等待下周的CPI、PPI、零售销售、美联储主席Warsh国会证词以及大型银行财报。周五本身没有足以决定新趋势的宏观数据，因此盘面更像是在期权到期、SK Hynix巨额新股供给和周末风险之间完成仓位整理。非投资建议。

主线：动量稳定，但领导权开始分散

当日的动量交易明显比周四复杂。存储股整体走弱，半导体大致持平，软件、科技硬件、网络与IT设备也相对落后；相较之下，银行、私募股权、信用卡、住宅建筑商、机械、建材、折扣零售和部分服饰股表现较强。Meta上涨约6%，成为大型科技股中最突出的个股，但大型科技整体并没有同步走强。

这意味着市场并未完全回到此前高度集中的AI硬件交易，而是在继续进行广度扩张。资金一方面保留了对AI资本开支的敞口，另一方面开始增加银行、住宅、机械、材料和消费周期股的配置。罗素2000下跌0.49%，说明这种扩张并不是无差别的风险偏好回升，而更偏向有盈利、资产负债表质量较高的周期性公司。

本周动量能够稳定，仍然支持一个基本判断：此前半导体和存储的大跌更多来自拥挤仓位、季节性和技术性去风险，而非AI需求发生根本变化。但周五存储走弱也说明，修复过程不会是单边的，资金需要消化SK Hynix上市带来的巨大新增供给和板块内部的重新配置。

Meta：模型效率成为新催化剂

$Meta 上涨约6%，核心催化剂是市场对Muse Spark 1.1的积极评价。SemiAnalysis认为，该模型在性能和价格之间形成了较强竞争力，推理成本大约只有部分前沿模型的四分之一，并将Meta视为前沿模型竞争中最可信的挑战者之一 。这与本周早些时候的14GW算力扩张、Iris自研芯片和持续加码资本开支，共同构成了一条更完整的逻辑。

过去市场对Meta的担忧主要有两个。第一，公司可能积累了过多算力，资本开支最终需要削减；第二，巨额投入未必能形成可变现的前沿模型。现在这两个问题都得到了一定回应：扎克伯格表示公司需要能够获得的全部算力，而Muse Spark 1.1开始展示较低成本的产品能力。
这不等于资本开支风险已经消失。算力翻倍意味着折旧、能源、网络、数据中心和芯片投入同步上升，短期利润率仍可能承压。周五股价的反应说明，市场暂时更愿意奖励模型进步和商业化潜力，而不是只盯着支出规模。

SK Hynix：供给冲击顺利落地

SK Hynix以149美元定价约1.78亿份ADR，总募资约265亿美元，定价较韩国市场折算价格溢价约3%，成为美国市场历史上规模第二大的股票发行之一。发行获得超过七倍认购，挂牌首日最终收涨，说明市场成功吸收了这笔巨大的股票供给 。

这个结果对存储链的意义不只是“Hynix上市成功”。它证明美国资金对HBM和DRAM龙头仍有很强的直接配置需求，但同时也会改变板块内部的资金流向。此前想获得Hynix敞口的资金主要通过韩国股票、EWY或其他亚洲科技基金实现，如今可以直接买入美元计价的SKHY，机构不再需要承担同样的市场准入和货币转换障碍。

因此， $SKHY 对其他存储资产存在两种相反作用：
正面作用：巨额发行被顺利消化，验证了AI内存的长期需求，也提高了整个HBM产业链在美国市场的可见度。
负面作用：部分机构可能卖出Micron、EWY或其他间接替代品，为SKHY腾出内存板块的配置额度。
中期作用：若SKHY逐渐进入美国半导体、AI和大型成长基金，可能形成持续的被动资金需求。

短期风险：新股定价、稳定价格机制和获配机构的仓位调整，可能让首周价格发现失真。

GraniteShares计划最早于7月13日推出SKHY的两倍做多ETF“SKUU”和两倍做空ETF“SKDD”，但仍以监管生效和正式上市为前提 。如果如期推出，短线资金、每日再平衡和杠杆产品对冲可能进一步放大SKHY的日内波动，因此不宜把首日交易表现直接当作长期定价结果。

存储：利好验证后转入再配置

SK Hynix上市前，市场一度预期整个存储板块都会受益；但周五实际表现是SKHY顺利落地、其他存储股相对偏弱。这恰恰符合“增量资产吸走部分旧仓位”的配置逻辑，而不是简单的行业利空。

Seagate逆势上涨2.3%，主要受益于富国银行将其评级从持有上调至增持。分析师认为近期回调改善了风险收益比，公司存在实现至少50美元每股收益的路径，并拥有较强的资本回报能力。它的走强说明存储链并未遭到全面抛售，市场正在区分HBM、DRAM、NAND和企业级硬盘各自的盈利周期。

对Micron而言，问题也不是基本面突然恶化。公司刚把美国长期投资计划提高至2,500亿美元以上，AI内存需求和产业扩张逻辑仍在。短期压力更多来自SKHY提供了一个估值、增长和产品组合都不同的新选择，机构需要重新确定Micron和Hynix在内存配置中的相对权重。

宽度：金融与周期接棒

银行、信用卡、私募股权、住宅建筑商、机械和建材周五跑赢，说明广度交易仍在继续。下周摩根大通、美国银行、花旗、高盛、富国银行和摩根士丹利将集中发布财报，市场提前交易投行业务、交易收入、贷款增长和资本回报改善的可能性。

银行财报面临的预期并不低。大型银行和区域银行指数今年都曾跑赢标普并创下纪录高位，强劲的投资银行业务和6月约2,000亿美元投资级债券发行也已经被市场部分计价。财报后的重点不是单纯看是否超过一致预期，而是观察：

投资银行和交易收入是否属于一次性高峰。
商业与工业贷款能否延续增长。
净息差是否接近阶段性顶部。
消费者信用质量是否继续稳定。
银行如何看待AI资本开支超级周期。

资金流也支持广度扩张。美国股票基金在截至7月8日的一周重新获得约251亿美元流入，全球股票基金流入约564亿美元，科技行业独占约188亿美元；AAII多空情绪差从前一周的负10.9个百分点改善至负0.9个百分点。资金并未离开科技，只是同时开始寻找科技以外的盈利来源。

宏观：市场进入数据等待期

周五没有重要经济数据，美联储最新货币政策报告也没有改变叙事。报告认为通胀仍然偏高、劳动力市场保持较强韧性，长期通胀预期大致稳定。市场对年内加息的定价升至约34个基点，较一周前增加约6个基点，说明投资者在股市上涨的同时，也在重新接受更长时间的高利率环境。

美债收益率当日上升，曲线呈熊市趋平，前端收益率上行约3个基点。此前3年、10年和30年期国债拍卖均获得良好需求，但强劲拍卖并未完全消除利率压力，因为市场还要面对企业债供给、财政风险、AI资本开支拉动的融资需求，以及美联储更偏鹰的反应函数。

下周CPI将是第一个真正的方向性催化剂。高盛预计6月总体CPI环比下降0.1%，核心CPI环比上涨0.17%，低于市场约0.3%的预期，理由包括新车和二手车价格回落、汽车保险走弱、住房通胀相对温和，以及旅行服务涨幅放缓。如果这一预测兑现，收益率可能回落并重新支持久期较长的成长股；如果核心数据达到或超过0.3%，当前约34个基点的加息定价还可能继续上升。

通胀：AI的双重作用

AI资本开支现在同时出现在多头和空头叙事中。对股票市场而言，Meta扩充算力、Amazon发债、Micron加大投资和SK Hynix巨额上市，都证明AI基础设施需求仍然强劲；但对美联储而言，同样的投资会推高电力、芯片、建筑、数据中心设备和高技能劳动力需求，可能提高中性利率并制造短期通胀压力。

纽约联储主席Williams表示，如果AI热潮持续且需求超过供给，可能形成新的通胀上行风险，甚至推高中性利率；但他的基准判断仍是AI最终会提高生产率，形成较大的正面供给冲击。这意味着AI对利率的影响具有时间错配：短期更像需求冲击，中长期才可能转化为生产率红利。
另一个值得关注的变化，是美联储偏爱的核心PCE指标可能获得约0.2个百分点的同比技术性下调。调整涉及投资组合管理与投资咨询、计算机软件及配件、法律服务等分项的计算方法。这可能在数据层面提供一定缓解，但属于统计方法变化，不能等同于真实需求和价格压力同步下降。

地缘与油价：风险降温但未消失

WTI原油周五下跌0.9%，即使本周美国与伊朗互相发动袭击、霍尔木兹海峡通行受限，油价仍没有延续突破。市场倾向于把当前局势理解为又一次“升级以促成降级”的循环，而不是全面战争重启。

特朗普表示伊朗希望继续谈判，但同时强调停火已经结束；伊朗方面否认下周将举行谈判，并表示如果美国违反谅解备忘录，将准备全面防御。双方表述互相矛盾，说明外交通道可能仍然存在，但协议本身已经非常脆弱。
市场真正关心的不是单次袭击，而是霍尔木兹海峡能否维持稳定通行。全球库存偏低、成品油价格仍高、俄罗斯炼厂又受到袭击，如果海峡长期中断，原油风险溢价可能迅速回归。周五油价没有上涨，只能说明市场暂时不相信冲突会持续升级，不能说明供应风险已经解决。

企业焦点：强基本面不一定带来上涨

Meta上涨6%，展示了高效模型与算力投入结合后可能产生的重新定价。Circle上涨5%，因为获得美国货币监理署最终批准、可以设立全国性信托银行；这提高了其在托管、结算和受监管数字资产基础设施中的战略地位。

WD-40上涨10.7%，三季度收入和每股收益均超预期，公司上调2026财年指引、毛利率同比扩大40个基点，并宣布1亿美元回购。Weyerhaeuser上涨4.2%，主要受益于评级上调，分析师认为其较资产净值的折价接近过去15年均值以下两个标准差。

Delta公布的二季度业绩和三季度每股收益指引均好于预期，并恢复全年指引，但股价仍下跌1.8%。原因是客运收入、单位收入和收益率不够强，市场此前对旅行需求、定价能力和高端客户定位的预期已经很高。这与Samsung利润大增但股价下跌属于同一类现象：财报季真正的风险不是盈利差，而是结果不够超过已经抬高的门槛。

财报门槛：盈利泡沫风险升温

FactSet的标普500二季度每股收益预测，在整个季度内上调了3.4%。作为对比，过去5年、10年和20年的平均变化分别是下调2.0%、2.7%和4.2%。这说明分析师不是像往常一样在财报前降低预期，而是在持续提高门槛。

Samsung的营业利润同比增长约19倍、股价却下跌近7%，是这种高门槛环境的直接例子。接下来的银行、Netflix和大型科技财报，也可能出现“数字很好，但不够好”的反应。尤其是那些已经因AI、资本市场业务或消费韧性大涨的公司，单纯超过一致预期未必足够，指引和未来利润率更重要。

市场目前更接近一个“预期脆弱”而非“经济脆弱”的状态。基本面仍然强劲，但估值、仓位和盈利预测已经把很多好消息提前计价，因此任何边际放缓都可能引发较大波动。

期权结构：净Gamma很高，但稳定体制尚未确认

7月10日收盘后的SPX期权结构出现了一个重要变化。SPX现价7,575.39，净GEX约正81.66亿美元每1%，与周四的正80.63亿美元接近；但Gamma翻转点从7,490上移至7,585，现价反而落在翻转点下方9.6点，约低0.13% 。因此，净Gamma虽然为正，长Gamma压制体制却没有得到确认。i

这与周四有本质区别。周四SPX站在7,490翻转点上方约53.6点，做市商处于明确的逢涨卖、逢跌买状态；周五翻转点大幅上移后，指数没有同步站上新的枢轴。市场并未进入真正的负Gamma环境，但已经从“稳定均值回归”退回“等待方向确认”。

Dealer Action Map仍显示强劲的下方承接。指数上涨1%时，做市商预计卖出约21.53亿美元；指数下跌1%时，预计买入约103.19亿美元，机械性下方承接约为上方卖压的4.8倍。

各到期Gamma也出现明显分化。0DTE GEX由周四的正17.57亿美元转为负5.24亿美元，意味着最短期限不再压制日内波动；但周度GEX大幅升至正39.47亿美元，月度为正13.33亿美元，季度为正8.75亿美元，LEAPS为正25.35亿美元 。中长期持仓仍然提供稳定性，但下一交易日开盘和盘中对消息会更加敏感。

IV30进一步降至12.02%，25-delta偏斜中的put溢价收窄至1.94%。这说明投资者购买下行保护的意愿继续下降，市场总体并不恐慌；但低隐含波动率与负0DTE Gamma组合在一起，也意味着如果下周CPI或银行财报带来意外，当前期权价格对实际波动的补偿可能不足。

结构解读：7,585和7,500决定下周开局

不要纠结文内一部分事实错误，我只想分享这篇内容的核心

市场真正交易的不是当下利润，而是利润能否持续；当牛鞭效应推动价格和订单被层层放大时，最好的财报往往意味着周期最接近拐点。

————————————

“鞭子的末梢，从来不知道鞭柄在做什么。

你站在鞭子的末梢，感受到的是音爆，是撕裂空气的速度，是那种被巨力裹挟着、身不由己的飞舞。

你以为自己在飞。你确实在飞。

但你不是在飞，你是在被甩。鞭柄只是轻轻抖了一下，手腕移动了十厘米。

你移动了三米，然后撞在墙上，断成两截。半导体行业就是这根鞭子。

在1996年的一个午后，一个惠普的采购经理在咖啡机前站了很久。

他盯着杯子里旋转的黑色液体，做了一个决定：多订30%的内存。不是因为订单增加了30%，而是因为万一明年缺货，他就是罪人。

他不确定未来会怎样。他唯一确定的是，如果别人都订了、他没订，那他就完蛋了。

他签下订单的那个瞬间，鞭柄动了一下。十二个月后，内存价格崩塌，全行业亏损数十亿美元。

那个采购经理，已经换了一份工作，在一家汽车零部件公司做供应链总监。没有人记得他。

但他是那场地震的第一块多米诺骨牌。这就是牛鞭效应。

它有一个更学术的名字，叫“长鞭效应”，或者“需求变异放大效应”。

用任何一个名字称呼它，它都是一样的东西：在一条供应链里，终端需求的微小波动，在向上游传导的过程中，被每一层逐级放大，最终在源头形成剧烈的震荡。

你不需要是一个经济学家才能理解它。你只需要想象一条鞭子：鞭柄移动十厘米，鞭梢移动好几米。

终端消费者的需求变动十厘米，存储芯片的价格变动好几米。不是线性关系，是杠杆关系。这条鞭子有几节。

第一节，是消费者。他们多买了几部手机，因为经济不错，因为年终奖发得比去年多一点。

他们不知道自己的购买行为会引发什么。他们只是想要一部新手机。

第二节，是零售商。他们发现库存消耗得比预期快，于是向经销商多下了订单。不仅要补足库存，还要为未来的增长预留缓冲。

第三节，是经销商。他们收到的订单增加了，于是向整机厂发出了更大的订单。他们开始担心：如果供货跟不上，客户就会去找别人。

第四节，是整机厂。他们看着堆在桌上的采购申请，做了一道简单的数学题：当前需求加上安全库存，再加上对未来的乐观预期，等于一个被放大了30%的数字。

第五节，是芯片设计公司。他们收到的订单已经面目全非，终端消费者的那一点点增加，在这里变成了庞大的、不容置疑的需求信号。他们必须抢产能，必须提前锁定供应，必须再多订40%。

第六节，是存储原厂——三星、海力士、美光。他们看着涌入的订单，发现供不应求。于是他们涨价。

涨价的消息顺着鞭子传回下游，下游恐慌了：现在不买，以后更贵。于是他们更疯狂地囤货。囤货制造了更多的“需求”，更多的“需求”推动更多的涨价。

这就是牛市。所有人的订单都建立在别人放大的预期之上，每一层都在为上一层虚构的需求买单，每一个人都以为自己是在为真实需求备货。实际上，他们是在为自己的恐惧备货。

但鞭子可以向上甩，也可以向下甩。当终端消费者的购买量只是轻微下滑——经济有一点疲软，手机少卖了几百万部——零售商发现，仓库里的货比预期多。

他们暂停下单，先消化库存。这个微小的停顿，传到经销商，订单减少。传到整机厂，订单取消。

传到芯片设计公司，订单归零。传到存储原厂，订单变成负数。原厂还在拼命生产——因为产线不能停，因为晶圆厂从投产到量产需要几个月——但仓库已经堆满了没人要的芯片。然后价格崩塌。

所有之前被放大了无数倍的虚假需求，在一瞬间消失。整个产业链开始漫长的去库存周期。

在这个周期里，那些曾经把利润推上巅峰的涨价，会反过来把利润推下深渊。

这就是半导体周期。它不是偶尔崩盘，它必然崩盘。它的崩盘不是基本面出了问题，恰恰相反——它的崩盘，是在基本面最完美的时候，突然降临的。

因为最完美的财报，意味着涨价已经到了极限，囤货已经到了极限，那个推动一切往上走的虚假需求，已经到了极限。
2026年7月7日，韩国首尔，汝矣岛。三星电子刚刚发布了人类历史上最炸裂的季度业绩预报：营业利润89.4万亿韩元，约合584亿美元，同比增长1810%。

这个数字超过了英伟达，超过了地球上任何一家公司单季赚钱的纪录。

半导体事业部负责人放话：“今年的营业利润将超过半导体事业开展40年来的累计利润。”两个小时后，股价暴跌近8%，触发熔断。

两周前，同样的剧本在太平洋对岸上演。美光科技交出了史上最强财报——营收414.6亿美元，净利润282.4亿美元，同比暴增1398%。毛利率84.9%，营业利润率81.2%。每一个数字都在证明，存储行业的黄金时代已经降临。第二天，股价暴跌10%。

再往前好多天，闪迪的财报同样炸裂。花旗昨天将目标价从1200美元上调至2200美元，高盛同步跟进。散户们看到“翻倍”两个字，冲了进去。然后，闪迪也崩了。

从6月19日到7月7日，不到三周。韩国KOSPI指数从9300点跌至7656点，跌掉了整个上半年涨幅的近三分之一。三星电子从374000韩元跌至293500韩元，跌幅21.5%。SK海力士从2980000韩元跌至2200000韩元，跌幅26.2%。美光从1250美元跌至944美元，跌幅24.5%。

这些下跌，发生在一个没有任何实质性利空的背景里。没有战争，没有金融危机，没有贸易制裁。多头们找不到凶手。
他们先是怪Meta——Meta说了一句“未来或许会出售算力”，一句棉花糖似的、它自己都不确定是什么意思的话。然后怪苹果——苹果把Mac和iPad涨价了，他们说这是苹果在压榨供应商。

最后怪三星的营收不及“最乐观预期”——1810%的利润增长，因为营收比那个从来没人确认过的“最乐观数字”低了1.7%，就成了利空。

这不是分析，这是在尸检报告上编造死因。一具尸体躺在那里，胸口插着一把刀，法医却指着尸体脚趾上的一块淤青说：看，这就是致死原因。

Meta的算力、苹果的涨价、1.7%的营收偏差——这些不是刀，这些是淤青。真正的刀，插在更深处。它叫牛鞭效应。

市场在怀疑的，不是这份财报的真实性，而是这份利润的可持续性。利润从哪里来？从涨价来。美光的产量在2026年基本持平，全球DRAM产能只增长了5%。

三星也一样。那1810%的利润增长，1388%的利润增长，来自哪里？来自价格标签被不断往上贴。在过去几个季度里，DRAM价格暴涨了超过100%。

但涨价的速度正在坍塌——从50%降到40%，从40%降到20%。

产能已经定死，出货量不可能暴增，利润增长就只取决于涨价速度。涨价速度在崩塌，利润的加速度就在崩塌。市场问的不是“这个季度赚了多少”，而是“下个季度还能赚更多吗”。

如果涨价从50%降到20%，利润增速就会从爆炸变成普通。如果涨价停止，利润就会停止增长。如果涨价逆转——那一天总是会来的，因为牛鞭效应的鞭梢已经开始往回甩了——利润就会雪崩。

到那时，美光的10倍PE会在瞬间变成50倍、100倍，不是股价涨了，是利润没了。那10倍PE，不是在说“这家公司很便宜”，它是在说“我不相信你明年还能赚这么多钱”。这是市场对周期性盈利的死刑判决。

但多头们有一个信仰。他们说，存储不是商品，存储是AI时代的必需品。所以它可以永远涨价，永远稀缺，永远被需要。但必需品，恰恰是最不能无限涨价的东西。粮食，是这个星球上最无可辩驳的必需品。

一个人可以不买手机，不买电脑，不碰任何电子产品，但他不能不吃粮食。但粮食能一直涨价吗？不能。当粮食涨到买不起的时候，人会饿死。

需求不会消失，但有效需求会瞬间归零——因为购买力被耗尽了。黄金，曾经被视为最坚不可摧的必需品。它是货币，是避险天堂，是人类几千年来最信任的价值储存手段。

黄金从5600跌到了3900，白银跌了超70%。原油，是现代工业的血液，是比存储芯片更不可或缺的必需品，它从120跌到了60多。没有任何商品可以逃脱经济周期，没有任何东西可以永远涨价。存储芯片，不是例外。

七巨头多数的现金流已经跌到了负，消费者已经因为涨价而停止购买手机和电脑，苹果已经公开抗议存储芯片涨价不合理。所谓“必需品”，只是一个被用来安慰自己的谎言。即使AGI实现，即使超级人工智能降临，也无法改变这个事实。因为AGI不会凭空印钞票，它不会让消费者突然多出一笔买手机的钱，它不会让云厂商的现金流从负六千亿变成正六千亿。它只是一个技术，不是奇迹。

鞭梢还在飞舞，但它已经开始减速。那些站在鞭梢上的人，还在感受风从耳边呼啸而过，还在以为自己正在飞向更高的天空。他们不知道的是，鞭柄已经在往回收了。从6月19日到7月7日，韩国从9300点到7656点。

三星从374000到293500。海力士从2980000到2200000。美光从1250到944。这是鞭梢在坠落前，画出的最后一道弧线。那些还在喊“黄金坑”的人，不是骗子，不是疯子。他们只是站在鞭梢上，风太大，听不见鞭柄往回收的声音。”

——Prometheus

本周美股走出了极端的“深V+绝地反弹”剧本。今日盘前，纳指期货微跌0.28%，道指期货微涨0.23%，标普500几乎平盘。由于本周后半程半导体涨幅过急，作为机构进行仓位周末优化的结算日，芯片股盘中将面临一定的获利了结压力；但鉴于昨日反弹情绪扎实，多头抱团锁仓过周末的意愿同样强烈。

VIX恐慌指数

@tparsi is using a real decline in the Strategic Petroleum Reserve to create a misleading sense of panic and suggest that the United States is “running out of options” against the Islamic Republic.

That conclusion is not supported by his graph.

His chart begins near a recent high, ends immediately after a deliberate emergency release, and truncates the y-axis at roughly 315 million barrels instead of showing zero or the full historical record. This visually exaggerates the decline and encourages readers to interpret a low reserve level as national helplessness.

Yes, the SPR fell to 319.5 million barrels by July 3, its lowest level since 1983. But the United States also held 411.4 million barrels in commercial crude inventories, bringing total crude stocks to 730.8 million barrels. The SPR is important, but it is not the entirety of America’s oil supply or energy capability. 

He also omits why the reserve declined. This was not an unexplained disappearance of oil. All 32 International Energy Agency members unanimously agreed to make 400 million barrels available after severe supply disruptions in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz. The United States committed 172 million barrels as part of that coordinated international response. 

Most importantly, much of the American release was structured as an exchange, not a permanent loss. Companies borrowing oil must return the barrels plus a premium. The United States has contracted to loan about 133 million barrels so far and expects to receive an average of 1.28 barrels back for every barrel released. Those returns are expected to begin later in 2026 and continue through 2028, potentially raising the SPR above 400 million barrels. 

Reuters also reports that reserve replenishment around the world could add approximately 506,000 barrels per day of crude demand in late 2026 and as much as 664,000 barrels per day by the third quarter of 2027. In other words, governments are already planning how to rebuild these reserves.

Parsi’s selectively framed oil chart answers none of those questions.

Crude oil prices plunged below $70 per barrel at the beginning of the week, and energy markets largely considered the Iran war over and done with as modest traffic flowed again through the infamous Strait of Hormuz.

But nearly 1 billion barrels of worldwide petroleum reserves are now depleted and not being replenished. At the same time, mothballed refineries have yet to come back online, China still hasn’t resumed importing large oil volumes, and now President Donald Trump has declared the interim peace deal “over” amid new drone and rocket exchanges.

The reality is there’s no clear, long-term peace deal in sight, even if a full resumption of conflict is avoided. That means the Strait of Hormuz is unlikely to return to its normal volumes for many months, and certainly not in time for the anticipated spike in demand when China and refiners start buying more oil again, energy analysts told Fortune. Prices are going to surge again—likely close to $90 per barrel—despite the world learning to adapt and avoid doomsday scenarios of $200 oil, they said.

And this could represent a nightmare scenario for a Trump administration eager to move on from Iran and lower fuel prices in time for the November midterm elections.

“There’s a bill that’s coming due,” said Marshall Adkins, head of energy for Raymond James, who admitted oil prices had fallen more than he anticipated. “The market thinks, ‘Oh yeah, things are going back to normal.’ But, watching Iran for as long I have, I don’t think that’s really going to happen. That hasn’t been the modus operandi for Iran for the last 45 years.”

If he didn’t before, Trump now knows. With the U.S. trying to increase traffic closer to the Oman side of the strait, Iran opened fire on some vessels this past week. The U.S. countered. At the July 8 NATO summit in Turkey, Trump said he thinks the interim deal with Iran is “over,” and he went on to call the Iranian leadership—in alphabetical order—“cancer,” “cheats,” “cuckoo,” “evil,” “liars,” “scum,” “sick,” “vicious,” and “violent” people. In June, he said they were “very rational people” who were “nice to deal with.” What changed? “I got to known them.” It also probably didn’t help that Israel warned the U.S. of a potential Iranian threat on Trump’s life.

Trump then also said he doesn’t think a full war would resume and that “anything that happens will be over quickly.” But there doesn’t seem to be any clear path to a peace plan in those words—even if he’s using them for negotiating leverage.

Adkins believes Iran will accept nothing less than a for-profit tolling system through the strait—it would be deemed a kind of service fee—and that Iranian control likely would keep traffic flows closer to half of their normal volumes. After all, most of the volumes flowing since mid-June were Iranian barrels. Typically, only about 10% of the traffic is from Iran. Even with more barrels redirected via pipelines—it will take at least a year for Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates to build new pipes—that would keep at least 5% of the world’s oil offline for many more months. “That’s still a big number.”

“The Iranians so far have been pretty successful at slow playing everything and wearing people out,” said oil forecaster Dan Pickering, founder of the Pickering Energy Partners consulting and research firm. “It doesn’t look like the regime is very weakened.”

But Pickering is more focused on China than Iran. That’s because China emerged as the so-called swing importer, cutting its world-leading oil imports by roughly 5 million barrels a day and leaning on its oil and fuel strategic reserves that also lead the world. Essentially, global oil production volumes have begun picking back up, but demand hasn’t, yet, which is why oil prices dipped more than anticipated. That is likely to change.

“We’re in this honeymoon phase where China hasn’t come back yet,” Pickering said, arguing that he expects China to start buying more barrels by the end of August, if not sooner. “That’s a biggie. China didn’t cut its consumption dramatically; China cut its imports dramatically. I think that’s what folks are not paying enough attention to.”

Math doesn’t add up

As oil prices fell after the interim peace deal was announced in mid-June, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz never rebounded to even one-third of its typically volumes. At the same time, shipping and insurance costs for oil tankers at least doubled.

Now, the U.S. has again revoked Iran’s waiver to sell its oil worldwide without sanctions. Still, the U.S. benchmark for oil only hovered near $71 per barrel on July 10 as energy markets remained optimistic.

There was a quick pivot in early July to a potential global oil glut—the same oil industry fear that existed early this year before the war—with production volumes rising in the Americas and now rebounding in the Middle East.

“The surprising thing is how quickly the narrative shifted from tightness to glut,” Pickering said. “The market moved this pretty quickly to a non-issue, and so far that has been the correct call. You have to respect the market forces that said, ‘This is all now done with,’ and see what comes next.”

But, even if it was the correct call, he said, it doesn’t mean it will remain that way much longer.

The U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve, for instance, is now at its lowest level since 1983, but it still holds more than 300 million barrels of crude, which is down from 415 million barrels at the beginning of the war.

And because Trump wants to keep fuel prices lower, there’s little chance the U.S. starts replenishing its strategic reserves before the midterm elections this year, analysts said. Trump has authorized the overall release of 172 million barrels over several months, so supplies could still dip much lower before they’re built back up, maybe beginning next year. But it will still require replenishment eventually.

Likewise, little-known Cushing, Oklahoma, is considered the nation’s oil storage and trading hub. The consensus is that commercial crude storage inventories are dangerously low when Cushing dips below 20 million barrels. Last week, Cushing fell to 19.6 million barrels, versus 33.5 million barrels two years ago, after hitting a 12-year low of 18.9 million barrels in mid-June. Below 20 million barrels, much of the remaining oil is counted as unusable tank bottoms, because storage tanks cannot be fully emptied to remain functional, or gunky sediment.

During the war, the U.S. exported record volumes of oil and refined fuel, which pushed refining margins to record highs this summer and kept prices at the pump from declining further as oil prices fell from an April high of $114 per barrel. The U.S. exports helped partially offset refineries that were mothballed in the Middle East and China, as well as those damaged from Ukrainian attacks in Russia. Adkins estimated close to 7 million barrels a day of global refining capacity had come offline.

“The Chinese refiners will say, ‘Hey, I’m making pretty good margin now. I’m going to turn back on.’ That’s probably starting to happen right now,” Adkins said.

How the world changes

The average price at the pump for a gallon of regular unleaded skyrocketed to a May high of $4.56 and has since fallen to $3.88 as of July 10, according to AAA.

That’s hasn’t really changed behavior or dramatically weakened oil demand, said Jim Wicklund, a veteran oil analyst and managing director at the PPHB energy investment firm. Electric vehicle sales may have jumped some, but there’s no evidence of a sharper fundamental shift beyond existing trendlines, he said.

“I think everybody was stunned at the world’s dependence on oil,” Wicklund said, noting that the effective closure of Hormuz was the greatest energy supply shock in modern history. “But it’s kind of like the U.S. dependence on Chinese [critical minerals]. Doing something about it is the hard part. Being incensed by the fact that that they own the supply chains is one thing; doing something about it is another.”

And that ongoing reliance should equate to at least a $5 per barrel geopolitical risk premium being baked into oil prices for the foreseeable future, he said, separate from any incoming demand surges.

Even if global oil demand is plateauing, it’s not going away for decades to come. The Middle East and OPEC—especially with the exit of the UAE and the threatened exit of Iraq—may be weakened going forward, but they’ll remain centerpieces of the energy world, said Arjun Murti, energy macro and policy partner at the Veriten research and investment firm.

“When people say there’s going to be permanent behavior change, that’s not going to happen on a three-month basis,” Murti said. “I believe [oil] demand will normalize back to the trend prior to this war.”

If anything, countries will be motivated to develop more of their own energy resources, he said, to avoid overreliance on imports.

“You have to manufacture stuff in your own country,” Murti said. “You must have some control over your energy sources and technologies.” If you don’t you remain at the mercy of geopolitics and individuals.
